data.title: XLB: Materials' Alpha Cools As The U.S.-Iran Conflict Presses On
data.description:
The resource trade was hot to begin 2026. Energy and Materials rose sharply, first on the back of gold and silver and then ahead of the conflict in Iran, as WTI and Brent crude oil climbed. But in the past two months, the State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) has struggled, both on an absolute and relative basis.

I had a hold rating on XLB back in November of 2025. Shares are up 20% since then, outperforming the S&P 500 by some 14%. Still, the valuation has risen, with most of the US market’s upward EPS revisions being seen in the Information Technology sector. With unconvincing technicals, I reiterate a hold rating. I’ll provide a fresh look at XLB’s fundamentals, valuation, and technicals.

XLB Now Lags SPY YoY

S&P 500 Sector Valuations

According to the issuer, XLB "seeks to provide precise exposure to companies in the chemical, construction material, containers and packaging, metals and mining, and paper and forest products industries." Tracking the S&P 500 Materials sector index, the fund allows investors to take strategic or tactical positions at a more targeted level than traditional style-based investing.

XLB is a large ETF with $7.5 billion in assets under management as of April 27, 2026. That’s up from $5.3 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low at just 8 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about 0.4 percentage points above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is solid right now, earning XLB a B ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Of course, Materials is among the riskiest and most cyclically sensitive corners of the stock market; hence, the ETF Risk Grade is a weak D+. Finally, on liquidity, the fund scores well, given an average daily volume of more than 16 million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just 2 basis points, per State Street Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots across the top two rows of the style box. It’s virtually entirely a mid- and large-cap product, with an ever-so-modest bent to value over growth. While there is no international exposure (being a US sector ETF), Materials equities tend to benefit from a weaker US dollar (like ex-US stocks). As plotted in the chart above, the greenback has been remarkably quiet in the past 10 months or so.

On valuation, XLB now trades at a below-market 19.0x price-to-earnings ratio. The earnings multiple is up a couple of figures since my November 2025 assessment, while the long-term EPS growth rate has actually ticked up. The PEG ratio is virtually unchanged, so I’m sticking with a hold rating.

XLB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, the 100% Materials sector product has high exposure to its top 10 positions. Linde plc (LIN) commands the highest weight, now close to 15%. Newmont Corporation (NEM) is a gold miner, and it has stumbled since notching highs earlier this year. Gold and silver have become highly tied to the S&P 500, so the diversification benefit from shares of companies in that industry is not all that solid right now.

Where XLB did help from a portfolio perspective was with its access to agricultural and fertilizer names during the Iran war outbreak. But even those stocks have cooled.

Holdings And Dividend Information

From a seasonal standpoint, XLB has tended to produce decent returns now through August. July has been particularly strong (second only to November in terms of average price gains over the past 10 years).

Bullish Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a fair valuation and concentrated allocation, XLB’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the ETF put in a record high back in the first quarter. A steep March drop reminded investors that risk can happen fast. Just a few weeks later, however, XLB successfully tested its long-term 200-day moving average, eventually returning to right near February’s range highs. The 200dma remains on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

But the ETF met selling pressure at the base of the previous range and now may be rolling over as the 50dma turns down. That’s not the ideal setup, despite a capitulation-like RSI figure at the March nadir. Long-term support is down just below $47, with resistance from $52 to $54. The volume profile interests me, since we have seen the number of shares traded fall as the consolidation has played out. That often means the previous trend (up) is still intact.

Fails At The February High, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on XLB. The Materials sector ETF is not all that compelling on valuation, and it has turned very sensitive to the conflict in Iran and domestic macroeconomic forecast changes. Its chart is also more of a show-me story than anything as we progress through the Q1 earnings season.

data.title: Nvidia: AI Transcends Semiconductors' Cyclical Nature
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is up by 21% since my last analysis of the company in March, in which I issued a Buy rating. Some argue that Nvidia is near a cyclical peak, but I think they will be proven wrong. I heard Elon Musk say something interesting the other day, and I'm paraphrasing strongly, but the notion was essentially AI growing in multiple s-curves, which I believe should logically reflect in the semiconductor companies, too.

It doesn't take a genius to see that we are roughly on the right-hand edge of the S-curve graph above with many AI semiconductor names.

That said, maybe the valuation can go higher in the short term, but it looks like we are hitting a bit of a sentiment threshold here. In the base and bull cases, we are in the period of diminishing returns of varying magnitudes, and in the bear case, we are approaching a digestion zone for hyperscaler capex. The latter of which may cause downside volatility temporarily, but the black line indicated above (representing the smoothed non-volatile trend) would likely just show a horizontal movement (the beginning of the next s-curve and the end of the last).

The AI era we have just entered macroeconomically is probably endless by necessity and via design. Higher productivity and margin uplift will keep demand for AI semis robust. The logical counter is digestion, not collapse.

There Is No Final Peak, Just Endless Waves

The first s-curve was training-capacity scarcity, and the next curves include inference, agentic workflows, sovereign AI, enterprise automation, robotics, and physical AI. As outlined above, Nvidia is probably on the right-hand edge of the first AI semiconductor s-curve, not the final one. A flattening in one deployment wave can look like a peak, but it may just be the horizontal transition before the next compute-demand curve begins.

The latest reported fundamentals (full-year revenue up 65%; net income of $120.1B) and the consensus forward long-term EPS CAGR (of 38%) don't show a "peak" equity. It might be the peak of multiple expansion, but not of equity price growth. Instead, it's more like the end of the explosive re-rate and the start of the s-curve steady compounding phases.

For Q1 FY2027, Nvidia's guide is ~$78B of revenue, explicitly assuming no China Data Center compute revenue. Gross margin is anticipated to be around 75%. That is not what a "peak" guide looks like.

Microsoft's (MSFT) latest quarter showed $37.5B of capex, with about two-thirds of that being spent primarily on GPUs and CPUs, which are short-lived assets (a key aspect of the long-term AI semiconductor growth horizon). Microsoft has said that customer demand still exceeds supply.

The primary risk with this monster capex is concentration risk, as such strong capex is primarily seen among the main hyperscaler Big Tech companies (MSFT is used here as the simple case study). That said, we should remember that Big Tech hyperscalers are essentially acting as compute funnels with one large input and many small outputs to diversified customers. AI is heavily proliferating across almost every vector of every industry. Just ask around.

Autonomous Economies Rise As NVDA Grows

The IMF has estimated that about 60% of advanced-economy jobs are AI-exposed and that about half could be negatively affected and the other half complemented. According to a gov.uk report, the UK is even more exposed at around 70% due to its service-heavy economy.

That noted, agentic AI is unlikely to result in fast mass firings. Instead, we're looking at hiring restraint, with companies likely cutting contractors, slowing graduate hiring, compressing support functions, reducing junior analytical headcount, and automating back-office workflows. The effect of this on companies is margin expansion, but delayed unemployment affects. This should largely create relative macroeconomic stability during the transition toward fully autonomous workflows, though it will not be without dips or volatility in broad-based consumer demand. Importantly, according to McKinsey, only 39% of respondents in its survey reported enterprise-level EBIT impact from AI so far.

The trend here is simple and very meaningful for NVDA shareholders: AI converts labor cost into compute demand. If AI agents, as I and many others expect, replace clerical, support, coding, research, compliance, and analytical labor, then corporate opex will migrate toward cloud, GPUs, memory, networking, storage, and model-serving. Obviously, this is very bullish for Nvidia's long-run demand pool.

I admit there is the risk here that the transition is not managed well, and workforce dynamics should and must be dealt with respectfully and with care, because advanced economies (and particularly the rich) may capture more AI capital returns, while BPO-heavy emerging markets may face export-service pressure. This mimics the industrial revolution and needs to be analyzed by policymakers and company strategists (from a long-term demand-stability standpoint, based on macroeconomic interconnection).

Britain's Industrial Revolution: from 1780 to 1840, output per worker rose 46%, while real wages rose only 12%, while from 1840 to 1900, output rose 90% and real wages 123%, turning wage disruption into broad abundance. Hopefully we can make AI a smoother ride from the outset.

Price Target

Nvidia doesn't look too expensive to me here, but it's not cheap after its recent post-Iran-conflict dip. My 12-month price targets are $250 per share in the bull case, $225 in the base case, and $200 in the bear case. This is based on simple sentiment analysis done on-chart and not overcomplicated with any more depth. Company financials are embedded in my price targets as reflected by sentiment derived from operational and macro analysis. I do not consider a cyclical crash as a likely near-term, or even medium-term, outcome.

Can Custom Silicon Crash NVDA? No.

Bear analysts have stated that custom silicon could impair NVDA's long-term bull thesis. Let's examine this. Amazon (AMZN) says Trainium and Graviton now have a combined annual run rate of about $10B, and Trainium2 is fully subscribed, with Trainium3 supply nearly committed by mid-2026. That's a clear example of the friction being outlined by bears.

But custom ASICs don't kill Nvidia; they just cap the monopoly premium. It's a moderate erosion to the market share, but it's not really a structural break at all, especially as the TAM is expanding. Excuse the profundity, but could the TAM expand infinitely? Intergalactically over millennia? As long as we don't blow ourselves up, intelligent compute could evolve forever, just like humans. Semiconductors will be at the core, and we are just at the dawn of AI's existence.

Hyperscalers will use ASICs for predictable, high-volume inference where cost matters most. But Nvidia will most likely remain dominant in frontier training, high-performance inference, rack-scale systems, networking, software, and workloads needing flexibility.

I would call the primary risk "capex digestion," not "AI demand collapse." Nvidia is not immune to cycles, but the cycle is moving from training to agentic inference, not from boom to bust.

Conclusion: Buy

The stock is up 21% already from my prior Buy rating. I'm a value trader and investor at heart, even if I trade on sentiment perceptions. If I owned Nvidia stock, I'd see no reason to sell here. It's not the ideal point to go "all-in" as the sentiment-based valuation is a little overextended, but I still see 20% 12-month upside in the bull case, and the long-term trajectory is deserving of a Buy. I see a collapse as a tail risk, so holding through the enduring growth horizon is not only intelligent to my mind, it is wise.

AI demand is probably endless by economic necessity, but semiconductor revenue will come in s-curve waves. It will look like build, digest, optimize, and rebuild. That means Nvidia may face volatility around capex digestion, but a durable upward trend will persist as labor, software, and industrial processes are progressively converted into compute.

data.title: Alkane Resources: Back On The Sale Rack
data.description:
Figures with A$ are in Australian Dollars. Figures without A$ are in United States Dollars. Costs and realized gold price figures are based on a flat 0.65 AUD/USD ratio and figures provided in United States Dollars at this ratio for a comparative basis to North American producers, in line with the three-year average (~0.65).

G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. OP = open-pit. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study. PFS = Pre-feasibility study. PEA = Preliminary Economic Assessment. NSR = net smelter return royalty or proceeds from smelter/refinery minus certain transport/smelter charges. GRR = gross revenue royalty. C&M = care and maintenance. NAV = net asset value. P/NAV = price to net asset value. EV/FCF = enterprise value to free cash flow.

Fiscal Q3'26 (March 2026 Quarter) Results

Alkane Resources Ltd (ALKEF) released its quarterly activities report last week, supplementing the brief update on its fiscal Q3 '26 (calendar Q1 '26) production results earlier this month.

Highlights were as follows:

  • A$275 million in revenue (+7% quarter-over-quarter)
  • Mine operating cash flow of A$189 million (+43% quarter-over-quarter)
  • Net cash and bullion up to A$342 million (+63% quarter-over-quarter)
  • Gold production of ~44,700 ounces, 377 tonnes of antimony produced, a new record.

Given the solid Q3 '26 (Q1 '26 calendar period) production, year-to-date production in the post-merger period (August 5th through March 31st) is sitting at ~117,400 ounces of gold and 768 tonnes of antimony vs. a guidance midpoint of 155,000 ounces of gold and 800 tonnes of antimony, leaving just ~37,600 ounces needed to meet its guidance midpoint and suggesting a high probability of meeting guidance and getting back on track after two misses (FY2024, FY2025) that ended an industry-leading 7-year streak of consecutive beats (FY2017 through FY2023).

As for concerns around fuel supplies, and as discussed in my previous updates, Alkane is far more insulated than its peers. This is because it has lower volume operations on balance, with one low-volume high-grade gold-antimony mine in Victoria (Costerfield), one relatively low volume UG operation in New South Wales (Tomingley) with an annual throughput of just over 1.0 MTPA, and a relatively small UG mine in Sweden, Bjorkdal (~1.0 MTPA mining rate).

All three operations run on grid power; none rely on diesel generators, and the hydro-electricity from Skellefteå Kraft AB (local power company) at Bjorkdal allows for ultra-low power costs at this mine. Hence, diesel requirements and diesel exposure are both modest, with Alkane also being unique because it benefits from extremely low royalties, with a weighted average royalty rate of barely 2.0% and ultra-low royalties at its Bjorkdal Mine of just 0.20%.

To put this in perspective, this is roughly 1/65th the royalty rate that producers in Ghana are paying currently (~13% including GSL) and closer to 1/80th the royalty rate in Turkiye. So, when one hears producers talking about rising costs because of royalties that are related to gold prices, Alkane is relatively shielded here, effectively meaning that it is capturing over $9.75/oz per $10.00/oz incremental move higher in the gold price vs. some peers that are capturing closer to $8.50/oz, i.e. losing 15% of any gold price to royalties right off the top before operating costs, etc.

Bjorkdal

Digging into the results a little closer, Bjorkdal had an excellent quarter, producing ~12,400 ounces of gold, with ~323,400 tonnes processed at 1.52 G/T of gold and ~90.4% recoveries. This was a material improvement from ~9,900 ounces in fiscal Q2 '26, benefiting from higher grades (1.52 G/T of gold vs. 1.04 G/T of gold), leaving Bjorkdal in a solid position to deliver on its guidance midpoint of 38,500 ounces of gold this year (11 month post-closing period), with ~28,300 ounces produced year-to-date.

As for cost performance, cash costs came in at A$2,506/oz [US$1,629/oz] while all-in sustaining costs improved to A$3,699/oz [US$2,404/oz], with greater leverage on fixed costs and despite higher sustaining capital spend in the period. As a reminder, Bjorkdal's AISC is higher this year because of significant sustaining capital investment, including increased capital development, new water management infrastructure, a major fleet replacement, and tailings dam construction.

Currently, this mine is dragging up Alkane's consolidated costs and some might see it as the proverbial eyesore in the portfolio from a cost standpoint, with it also looking relatively insignificant in terms of its production profile at just ~40,000 ounces (44,000 ounces including one month pre-merger closing).

However, the combination of reduced sustaining capital spend later this year and much higher production over the coming years will see costs drop materially at Bjorkdal, with expected production of ~51,000 ounces per year over the next five fiscal years, well above fiscal 2026 levels. So, although this mine might seem like a drag today and less core of an asset, this is hardly the case, and it's in an exceptional little asset with a very enviable position given its ultra-low power costs and among the lowest royalty rates and corporate tax rates sector-wide.

Further, this asset is permitted for up to 1.7 MTPA (over 20% above the current throughput rate), drilling results at the nearby Storheden deposit have been exceptional and well above resource grades (~99,000 ounces at 1.74 G/T of gold or ~1.77 million tonnes) with highlights like 0.25 meters at 142 G/T of gold, 0.25 meters at 111 G/T of gold and 0.60 meters at 16.2 G/T of gold (all true widths) and offers an opportunity to displace lower-grade stockpiles at ~0.60 G/T of gold with relatively higher-grade UG ore.

In addition, drilling at the Lake Zone Skarn has also shown exceptional grades over solid widths, including highlights of ~4.9 meters at 16.2 G/T of gold, ~0.85 meters at 53.5 G/T of gold and ~3.2 meters at 34.2 G/T of gold offers the potential to spike feed grades as well. Finally, North Zone drilling, where a mining concession is under application, also showed strong results, including ~0.42 meters at 157 G/T of gold, ~0.49 meters at 40.1 G/T of gold (all true widths).

Hence, not only do I see the ~10-year mine life here massively understating this asset's long-term reserve potential (near-mine opportunities, plus Storheden, plus small resource at Norrberget, a high-grade open-pit resource of ~317,000 tonnes at 3.54 G/T of gold), but I think there's upside to this production profile given excess capacity, ability to optimize the grade profile (displacing lower-grade OP tonnes and/or low-grade stockpiles).

For this reason, I don't see any reason to discount this asset just because it's small or look at it as non-core, especially given its incredible history of reserve replacement and long production history (producing since 1988).

Costerfield

As for Costerfield, this gold-antimony mine produced ~10,600 ounces of gold and ~400 tonnes of antimony in fiscal Q3'26, with higher throughput (~35,600 tonnes vs. ~34,700 tonnes) partially offset by modestly softer grades (10.21 G/T of gold vs. 10.44 G/T of gold in fiscal Q2 '26), but still miles above the industry average.

This has pushed year-to-date production to ~27,100 ounces of gold and ~800 tonnes of antimony and is tracking to meet its target of 39,000 ounces of gold and 800 tonnes of antimony at the mid-point, with antimony well ahead of planned levels and ~11,900 ounces needed to meet gold production guidance, with the potential for production to come at the low end of the range (37,000 to 41,000 ounces).

While fiscal 2026 has been a resounding success from an exploration standpoint, Alkane did share that some mining areas didn't perform as well as expected in its forecasted models because of grade variability, even if mining and milling rates have exceeded planned rates. This is not the end of the world, and it's not abnormal to see this occur at a narrow-vein mine like Costerfield.

And while 2021 grades outperformed plans (+3% gold, +3.4% antimony on resource ounces) and tonnes outperformed in the 2022 through 2023 period, grades have been noisy, similar to Agnico Eagle (AEM) Fosterville Mine nearby that also regularly sees a swing in positive and negative grade reconciliation.

Hence, I don't think that this commentary on grades should be taken as alarming at all, and Alkane has noted that it is continuing to work on drill/blast optimization, enhanced operator training and the transition to emulsion explosives in an effort to improve mining recovery and reduce mining dilution.

As for cost performance, this relatively small mine continues to operate at costs at or below the industry average and cash costs improved to A$1,567/oz [US$1,018/oz], while all-in sustaining costs came in at A$2,521/oz [US$1,639/oz], below the industry average and affected by higher sustaining capital in the period.

As for exploration, Alkane continues to deliver impressive results from near-mine drilling, including ~1.04 meters at 132.2 G/T of gold and 19.8% antimony and ~1.22 meters at 267.5 G/T of gold and ~5.6% antimony at Kendall, as well as a moderately thicker intercept of ~1.92 meters at 18.2 G/T of gold and ~11.9% antimony.

These are incredibly high-grade hits when incorporating antimony credits as well and even factoring in additional mining dilution, ~1.04 meters at 132.2 G/T of gold and ~19.8% antimony is near $20,000/tonne rock values in this highlight hit vs. ~$160/tonne mining costs, making this a highly attractive zone for future mining.

As noted by the company, development to access the extended Kendall system began late last year, and 25 individual veins have now been identified and modeled, sitting immediately above its Youle/Shepherd ore bodies which are being mined, with the 520 vein being traced over 500 meter strikes and a 200 meter vertical depth. Meanwhile, at the 501 vein, some hits, while narrow, exceed 50% antimony, including ~0.44 meters at 54.2 G/T of gold and 50.8% antimony.

Importantly, Kendal is not the only area that is churning out incredibly high-grade results, with Brunswick South also delivering impressive results that included ~0.44 meters at 76.6 G/T of gold and ~1.58 meters at 52.3 G/T of gold prior to the merger closing.

Like Kendal, Brunswick South also lies closer to existing UG infrastructure (270 meters south of existing development), can easily be brought into the mine plan and lies between the Cuffley deposit and previously mined Brunswick deposit (mined in the 1990s and again in 2018). Further, there is an additional opportunity at True Blue, 2 kilometers from its current mining area and showing similar mineralization to August/Cuffley, deposits that were mined from 2008 to 2018.

To date, True Blue's resource sits at ~61,000 ounces of gold and ~4,500 tonnes of antimony but comes in at much higher grades (13.1 G/T of gold, 3.1% antimony) and could add well over a year of mine life at superior grades to its current mine plan. However, if True Blue is anything like the Central Corridor that hosts multiple deposits (Brunswick, Augusta, Cuffley, Youle, Shepherd), there is much more to come, with Alkane's CEO noting on the most recent conference call:

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"...indications are that we may be able to replicate the entire corridor length that we see all the way, Augusta, Brunswick, all the old mines that have pulled over 1.0 million ounces out at Costerfield in the past.."

- Q2'26 Conference Call, Alkane Resources (emphasis added).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As for deeper drilling at its high-grade Costerfield Mine, Alkane noted that the sub-King Cobra drilling program has been put on hold with the priority being infill drilling on Brunswick South. Sub King Cobra (Sub-KC) hosts a relatively small inferred resource of ~155,000 tonnes at 7.0 G/T of gold and 0.8% antimony for ~35,000 ounces of gold and lies directly beneath the Cuffley Mine.

However, the two largest opportunities that are not in Alkane's valuation today and can't be quantified are as follows:

1] a baby-Swan Zone, similar to what Kirkland Lake Gold uncovered at Fosterville, which is barely 40 kilometers northwest of Alkane's Costerfield Mine. This zone was a game-changer for Fosterville with 1.0+ ounce per tonne grades, and for a company like Alkane, a discovery like this could blow the lid off the share price, but for now is merely an upside or "dream" scenario for this mine.

2] Sunday Creek style mineralization, which Alkane is testing for, and which has earned Southern Cross Gold (SXGCF) a $2.0+ billion market cap as a pre-resource company, with its project sitting just south of Alkane's Costerfield Mine.

To be completely clear, we can't quantify this upside today and I've assigned no value for it, but it's certainly an enviable position to be in with one of the highest-grade gold mines globally, an antimony kicker that allows for well below average unit costs vs. mines of a similar size and a wide-open land package where the southern neighbor is uncovering a high-grade multi-million ounce resource and your northwestern neighbor is Agnico Eagle, and where millions of ounces of gold have been produced, with 2019 and 2020 grades coming in at over 30 G/T of gold, with cumulative production of ~1.26 million ounces of gold in just these two years.

Tomingley

As for Alkane's largest mine, Tomingley, this mine produced ~21,700 ounces of gold in fiscal Q3 '26 at A$2,444/oz [US$1,589/oz] all-in sustaining costs and has produced over 62,000 ounces year-to-date, tracking well against guidance of 77,500 ounces at the mid-point (80.1% of guidance delivered in first three quarters). Alkane noted that UG ore mined was slightly below plan, with some stopes requiring rework, but this was offset by higher development ore tonnes. On a positive note, milling rates have exceeded plans and throughput rates have improved to ~1.3 MTPA with pre-crushing, with the company noting it should be able to get closer to ~1.4 MTPA.

As shown below, the Newell Highway Realignment Project to deliver open-pit tonnes next year is underway and due for completion in the first half of 2027, unlocking a high-grade open-pit ore source, San Antonio, next year. This will allow for only slightly lower grades than Roswell UG (~2.5 G/T of gold) but mined by open-pit, setting up considerable free cash flow generation for this asset over the coming two years, especially with capex rolling off from the Highway Realignment Project, with growth capital at Tomingley of ~US$30 million this year that is non-recurring.

Like its other two mines, though, Tomingley has immense upside and sits just north of Northparkes, a copper-gold mine that Evolution Mining is looking to expand. For starters, Tomingley sits on the northern end of a ~15 kilometer belt with past producing at Peak Hill (southern end of belt, near Northparkes) where Alkane is testing for gold-copper porphyry potential. Meanwhile, Alkane is also testing for low sulphidation epithermal gold targets at Glen Isla, east of its Tomingley Mine, informed by an IP survey completed last year.

However, aside from these regional targets, there is considerable upside near-mine, suggesting material growth in the resource base over the next few years and a meaningful extension in its mine life. For starters, drilling at El Paso just south of San Antonio (two new high-grade open pits set to be brought online) hit ~8.2 meters at 3.7 G/T of gold, ~32.1 meters at 1.65 G/T of gold, ~14 meters at 1.71 G/T of gold and ~17 meters at 4.02 G/T of gold, with Alkane working to build out a resource here just over 5 kilometers from its plant.

More recently, though, Alkane has confirmed high-grade mineralization in the Roswell UG Western Monzodiorite with hits that included 5.9 meters at 31.0 G/T of gold, 17.4 meters at 4.3 G/T of gold and 3.5 meters at 16.1 G/T of gold (currently not in reserves). It also tested a seismic reflector ~390 meters below current resources and while no major intercepts were delivered, the discovery and confirmation of mineralization at this deeper target is encouraging, with highlight hits of 2 meters at 5.9 G/T of gold, 0.3 meters at 11.4 G/T of gold and 7.8 meters at 1.15 G/T of gold.

Alkane stated the following in relation to this new target:

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

" This reflector, identified in three 2D seismic traverses, is dipping ~30 degrees to the east with more than a 5km strike length, is interpreted to be a major fault or conduit for the upwelling of gold-arsenic enriched hydrothermal fluids to the Tomingley gold system."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Finally, and while also not at all reflected in Alkane's valuation, the company made another discovery during the sector-wide correction last year (November 3rd, 2025), with ~26 meters at 4.36 G/T of gold, ~10.7 meters at 2.09 G/T of gold, and ~7.8 meters at 3.46 G/T of gold at McLeans, which is an entirely inferred resource of ~70,000 ounces at 2.51 G/T of gold.

While drilling to increase confidence in this ore body (tightening drill spacing to 40 x 60 meters), a new second andesite was discovered, ~150 meters west of the current resource, while drilling into the eastern andesite delivered solid grades, confirming mineralization here (~8 meters at 2.67 G/T of gold, ~8 meters at 4.38 G/T of gold).

In summary, I don't see any reason to believe that Alkane can't add another 600,000+ of resources between near-mine and regional targets over the next few years, in addition to converting a large portion of resources (~1.47 million ounces of gold at Tomingley, plus ~108,000 ounces separate at Peak Hill), which should ultimately extend Tomingley production into the late 2030s.

This is helped by what is a far more favorable gold price environment as well, allowing Alkane to relax cut-off grades a little vs. its current reserves, which are based on a gold price of A$2,350/oz [US$1,530/oz].

Boda-Kaiser

Finally, as for Boda-Kaiser, this asset continues to get more valuable if we are in a higher for longer gold price environment. In fact, if we assume a flat $4,200/oz gold price (where gold made its recent lows), far more conservative capex/costs that bake in inflationary pressures vs. its scoping study and a $10,500/tonne copper price, Boda-Kaiser's estimated NPV at an 8% discount rate comes in at ~$1.14 billion. This is despite being discounted heavily, as first production is at least six years away.

However, the value of this project increases materially each year, with the NPV (8%) growing to ~$2.2 billion [US$1.60/share] using the same assumptions in 2032 without the six years of discounting until first cash flows, and assuming no upside in gold/copper prices ($4,200/oz gold, $10,500/tonne copper), all while using the same conservative capex/cost assumptions.

The best thing that Alkane can do is continue advancing this asset, test for new porphyry potential to ensure it doesn't under-price the value of any joint-venture if a partner does come in and be patient on partnering, given that the value of this asset will be higher as it gets closer to cash flows (de-risked) and the discount to these cash flows is reduced later this decade when it comes time to consider a financing and final investment decision.

Today, I am valuing this asset at just US$445 million or US$0.32/share ($3,500/oz gold, $10,500/tonne copper, 8% discount rate, discounting starts immediately and 0.60x NAV multiple) in my fair value for Alkane of US$1.66, but this asset could arguably cover today's whole market cap alone once permitted.

Alkane's CEO Nic Earner had the following to say about this asset in a recent interview, which I could not agree more with, in the sense that it is not represented in the stock's valuation at all today as it sits at US$1.15.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"So clearly, it was a 24% IRR at half the gold price (US$2,330/oz gold). This is a project that will execute in this environment. So this is all about the approvals. This is the size of our tenement. We continue to look for 'is there a higher grade portion that's better than 0.60% gold-equivalent that we can feed in?'

Of course, this is a grind; we absolutely get that. But we have been in this region for the better part of fifty years, and when we get this approved, it will be the fourth major approval we've had done in 13 years. If you look at our share price vs. our peers in Australia, you will see this carries very value. So this is the most leveraged thing we can do to our market cap."

Alkane Resources CEO, Nic Earner, April 2026 (emphasis added).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Shareholder Returns

Finally, on shareholder returns, Alkane is set to exit FY2026 (June 30th, 2026) with US$300 million in net cash and bullion, and it continues to be of the view that its priority is to look for internal ways to create shareholder value, including re-investing in the business, expanding its resources and/or look at inorganic growth opportunities if accretive opportunities come up.

However, with the speed at which cash balances are rising, there's no way it can put capital to work quickly enough, even if it's spending US$100 million/year in capex. This is because at the current rate Alkane will exit FY2027 with US$530 million in net cash (June 30th, 2027), with this cash balance growing further to US$800+ million [US$0.58/share] at June 30th, 2028, all at a $4,600/oz gold price assumption.

Clearly, this provides the opportunity for a base dividend, which could easily come in at US$0.024 - US$0.032/share annualized (~$34 - ~$44 million or 13% to 17% of free cash flow in calendar year 2027), translating to a very attractive forward yield. The other option is share buybacks, which could certainly help to put a floor under the stock and would be an excellent way to grow free cash flow per share in this period where the full power of this portfolio isn't evident as it still delivers into the last of its partial hedges (~20% of production).

Personally, I would prefer to see Alkane focus on growth, and like Discovery Silver (DSVSF), I think this is the best way to create shareholder value. However, if the company can't find an inorganic opportunity to buy a developer and put that cash towards a mine build, there's certainly the opportunity for a solid base dividend here as well by mid-2027.

So, with a strong pipeline (Boda-Kaiser), a balance sheet and cash flow profile that could easily support a new mine build if Alkane finds an advanced developer to merge with and a deep level of undervaluation even if Alkane just chugs along at a ~180,000 ounce production rate and keeps building cash, I continue to see Alkane as one of the four most attractive ways to put cash to work in the sector today, with other names I have recently added to discussed here at Alluvial Gold, in addition to all top miners ranked with their fair value estimates at different gold price sensitivities.

M&A

As for M&A, Alkane has provided more color on what it's looking for, and there is the potential that we could see the company transact at some point over the next 18 months. In my view, this would be a positive for the company, given that it would provide it with greater scale, another asset of Tomingley or greater size to further de-risk the business and weather any cycles in the gold price and also diversify it further from a cash flow basis.

Alkane has made clear it is not looking for a major permitting undertaking and its ideal setup would be something that's producing or could be in production by 2028 latest. Some might worry that this could be expensive in the current environment, but I think the opportunity here is that it could look at what another producer considers non-core, but is very core to Alkane, with 70,000+ ounces clearly moving the needle and having the potential to be bought cheap.

The other opportunity is looking at partnering with a developer, which would see its shares re-value much more quickly under Alkane as a cash-flowing producer with operating experience than on its own.

Importantly, Alkane will not sacrifice on jurisdiction and is looking in Canada, USA, Scandinavia, Australia, and/or New Zealand, meaning that there is no worry of Alkane losing its Tier-1 status.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"We're going to deliver on our plans. Absolutely, we're in the market for other opportunities; we'd very much like to extend our business. We would very much like to add another mine somewhere in the Australia, New Zealand, North America, Scandinavia space. Somewhere around the 70,000 to 120,000 ounces. Material for us, but without being so much that other people want it. That is important because we want another inorganic growth opportunity in between."

- February 2026 Presentation, Alkane Resources.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Valuation

Based on ~1.38 billion shares and a share price of US$1.09, Alkane trades at a market cap of ~$1.50 billion and an enterprise value of ~$1.26 billion. To put this in perspective, Alkane's neighbor, Southern Cross at its Sunday Creek Project in Australia trades at a 20% higher market cap than Alkane despite having no production, no resource, and just promises of a future mine at some point next decade. Obviously, there is certainly value in multi-million ounce deposits in Central Victoria, and Costerfield is clearly smaller by scale than Southern Cross's opportunity.

Still, Alkane's Costerfield Mine is already operating and will have generated $300+ million in mine-site free cash flow from this asset alone, let alone heaps of free cash flow from its two other mines (Tomingley, Bjorkdal) before Sunday Creek pours its first ounce of gold, if it ends up being a mine. Plus, Alkane is permitted, has an established resource and has locked up additional ground in Central Victoria at Nagambie (recent JV announcement).

So, for those willing to buy permitted and established operations over speculation and ounces that might get delivered several years into the future, there is a clear relative value proposition here in Alkane vs. Southern Cross, never mind several of the other 100,000 to 500,000 ounce producers in the sector today.

So, why the discount?

1) Today, I believe the market is under-pricing the value of Bjorkdal because of its higher costs. Addressing this point, Bjorkdal is a smaller mine, which impacts its costs, but Alkane is working to push production towards ~60,000 ounces per annum with the potential for further growth from satellite opportunities, leveraging fixed costs at this asset, which has an incredible track record of reserve replacement and a 10-year life.

Besides, FY2026 is being impacted by elevated sustaining capital for increased capital development mining, new water management infrastructure, and tailings dam construction, as well as a major fleet replacement on site.

Hence, even absent a sustained increase to ~60,000+ ounce per annum production levels (which are expected later this decade anyway during higher grade years), all-in sustaining costs at Bjorkdal should decline materially starting this fall and through FY2027, highlighting that there is no reason for cash flows from this steady producer that's been operating for several decades in a safe jurisdiction with low-cost hydropower, low taxes and ultra low royalties to be discounted.

2) Second, some might be worried about Costerfield, with these seen as a smaller and potentially shorter life operation. I would argue that this view is nonsensical and this asset should actually trade at a premium, being north of a major new discovery in Victoria (Sunday Creek), being east of Fosterville (Agnico Eagle's only Australian mine and one of the highest-grade mines globally), and being the most significant western antimony producer, a highly critical metal. However, we can address the reserve worries below:

For starters, Costerfield has a ~150,000 ounce reserve base with its mine life extending into Q1 2030. However, this asset has consistently replaced reserves, has an additional ~1.02 million tonnes of material outside of reserves in the measured, indicated and inferred categories (including the high-grade True Blue deposit directly west), and its reserves today are based on a conservative US$2,100/oz gold and US$16,000/tonne antimony price and 5.6 G/T gold-equivalent cut-off.

Hence, there is material room to relax cut-off grades by taking gold prices slightly higher to bring in additional reserves if needed, never mind the fact that there's room for material resource conversion at True Blue and other targets even holding cut-off grades where they are. Further, Nagambie offers the potential for trucking high-grade ore to Costerfield, which could offer mine-life extension and/or modest organic growth.

For those unfamiliar, Nagambie's ~320,000 gold-equivalent ounces at near 19.0 G/T gold-equivalent is the highest-grade resource I'm aware of in Australia and one of the highest-grade resources globally (3.3 G/T of gold, 3.9% antimony), with this being an antimony-dominant resource with gold coming second. Hence, I see this as an incredible deal for Alkane to take advantage of a less well-capitalized explorer that's a neighbor at its operating mine and put itself in a position to earn in on this asset for a mere ~$20 million or barely one month of free cash flow generation, at its election (assuming it likes what it's seeing during the earn-in process).

3) The third reason for a discount is that the market likely sees Boda-Kaiser as needing approvals, needing a partner, and being several years out from the market willing to assign much value to this asset. And while the latter point is certainly fair, the arguments for a discount are simply not the valid with Costerfield nor Bjorkdal.

Further, while Boda-Kaiser may not get much value today, investors aren't paying for any value here. They're buying a tri-asset gold producer for ~$1.3 billion enterprise value that's set to exit calendar year 2026 with over $300 million in net cash/bullion that's trading at just over 3.4x calendar year 2027 free cash flow estimates.

Medium-term, and assuming Alkane can continue advancing Boda-Kaiser and/or secure a partner to develop this asset, I would expect a massive re-rating in the stock, but it's quite clear that this asset is free in the valuation today.

Summary

  • Alkane Resources trades at a market cap of ~$1.50 billion and an enterprise value of ~$1.26 billion, a ridiculously cheap valuation for a tri-asset producer in solely Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions that should generate ~$260 million in free cash flow next year in calendar year 2027 ($4,500/oz gold price assumption).
  • While Alkane may be a ~180,000 ounce per annum producer on a gold-equivalent basis (including an antimony kicker, a critical metal) which is relatively smaller, it has 420,000 ounce per annum potential long-term with its Boda-Kaiser gold-copper project having the ability to produce ~159,000 ounces of gold and ~35,600 tonnes of copper in its first five years (2032 start?) or ~250,000 gold-equivalent ounces per year
  • Given the distance to first production at Boda-Kaiser, Alkane is open to accretive M&A that could lift its production profile in the medium-term, but has been clear it will focus on solely Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions and advanced assets (i.e. close to production or producing) if it completes a transaction
  • Further, Alkane is open to joint-venturing at Boda-Kaiser given that this is a relatively large project from a capex standpoint, but even at a 60/40 split, Alkane would still have 330,000+ GEO potential (~89% growth) from current levels, in addition to the ability to share capex and a large cash inflow to Alkane for any prospective partner in order to earn in on this project

With a large-scale gold-copper project in the wings that sits north of Newmont's (NEM) Cadia Mine, a large land package with the potential for an additional porphyry and new gold deposits (15-kilometer belt at Tomingley, large land package in NSW between two majors), also including Evolution Mining (CAHPF) and solid exploration success at all of its assets, investors are arguably getting optionality on Boda-Kaiser and new regional discoveries for free given that they're only paying ~3.4x calendar year 2027 free cash flow for a tri-asset producer in Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions.

Hence, I see this pullback in Alkane Resources below US$1.10 as a gift. Further, as its technical chart shows, Alkane looks to be building a handle and higher low that should land in the US$1.08 - US$1.10 region after breaking out to new highs earlier this month and now pulling back into the range to build a right side to this base.

From a bigger picture standpoint, Alkane has broken out of a 10+ year-base to new highs and its highest levels it traded at since 2012, with considerable upside given that this is a rare case of deep value (~3.5x calendar year 2027 EV/FCF estimates) and momentum, with it now having confirming a new quarterly breakout with all of its key moving averages trending higher.

Overall, I would not be surprised to see Alkane hit US$2.00 within the next 18 months, and I see no reason for this discounted valuation when Alkane checks multiple boxes, including:

1] gold and critical metal exposure (gold/antimony).

2] Tier-1 only ranked jurisdictions (Australia, Sweden).

3] an industry-leading track record of beats/misses vs. guidance (~10.7% average beat in 2017-2025 period).

4] immense exploration upside in prolific regions, surrounded by Agnico Eagle and Southern Cross at Costerfield, Bolliden at Bjorkdal and Newmont/Evolution at Tomingley/Boda-Kaiser.

5] a clean and extremely strong balance sheet, with Alkane set to exit its June 2026 quarter (fiscal Q4 '26) with ~US$300 million in net cash/bullion.

data.title: PayPal Profit Engine Could Be Threatened By Agentic Shopping Revolution
data.description:
The case here is mostly qualitative, concerned about PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) with respect to how future-proofed its value-add is if (and let's be honest, probably more of a "when") agentic shopping becomes a viable offering at some point in the relatively near future.

Our view is that the PayPal checkout button (branded merchant volumes) is primarily valuable on account of the fact that PayPal can demonstrate to vendors like e-commerce platforms that the presence of their checkout button reduces cart abandonment and increases cart size due to the trust that having a PayPal pay option affords, avoiding the need to hand over credit card info and also speeding up the checkout process.

If shopping becomes intermediated by an AI, and the AI gathers the basket in one place, this could lead to scenarios where human eyes no longer really visit the pages anymore where checkout occurs - which is a possible but by no means assured utility for the space - which would be a significant erosion of the value-add that justifies the considerable margin that vendors have to give up when adopting PayPal checkout. Without trying to scry the specific future scenario, the point is that it is possible that agentic AI shopping tools would be the new locus of trust, less so downstream on e-commerce checkout pages, and none of us can predict for sure that PayPal will be wholly unaffected by the advent of LLM-based AI or will somehow be able to adapt.

In addition to that, there is sophisticated and credible lateral competition to the current checkout button, with other wallets gaining critical mass and able to partner with merchants effectively to offer this less differentiated feature.

Therefore, in the spirit of capital preservation, we reserve these concerns and focus funds on other ideas that may be more unfairly discounted, though we recognize that a pretty grim scenario, basically of secular decline, is already reflected in PayPal's valuation in the FCFY teens.

Latest Results

The latest Q4 results appear to us to reflect lower average takes across the board. Payment volumes are up ahead of revenues. Operating income growth is also decelerating. This has likely factored into the downward rating of the stock.

Apparently branded checkout has been an area where the company feels they've underperformed. This refers specifically to the check-out button as opposed to the backend merchant solutions in the unbranded business that works with the gamut of popular ways to pay. It explains some of the evidence of lower average takes and also somewhat underwhelming profit growth, with margin, at least on a non-GAAP basis (adjusting for some acquisition amortization and restructuring charges in each of the years), getting a bit crimped despite the single-digit organic growth and the help of operating leverage.

The company is targeting a higher level of organic growth towards 7% that they hope to reach with growth in Pay with Venmo and debit cards (consumer), but some recurring factors are not going to recur, such as BNPL price increases that were part of the 2025 picture. So it won't be particularly easy to achieve, though the company will surely do whatever it can to increase efficiency.

Forward Threats

But still we have concerns.

The initial threat is from lateral competition, mainly on the branded merchant volumes. Other wallets like Apple Pay and Revolut (a bank now in some jurisdictions, we believe) are both capable of offering the same checkout trust value as PayPal with its branded offering, as those wallets also have reached critical mass. The take rate on branded processing, by some estimates (from Mizuho (MFG) being the most authoritative), is almost 10x the take on the unbranded (figures were 2.25% for branded, 0.3% unbranded, closer to what take rates probably are P2P), so competition could drive commodification that could hurt PayPal's unit economics and drive negative mix effects on growth. Revolut might be offering significantly lower rates, 0.5% + 0.02 GBP, which is much lower than PayPal's take rate and headline fees. These are tough competitors. If the take rates are what we just said above on each type of traffic, the gross 1.65% take rate (transaction revenues/TPV) implies that branded, which must have the highest take rate other than Xoom (which is not an overwhelming proportion given that cross-border TPV isn't that high at 12% and is not only comprised of Xoom but also plenty of P2P), is the single largest contributor to overall takes from transactions. This appears to be supported in the earnings presentation, where the TPV proportion of branded is at 34%, with what we assume are significantly higher take rates than the company average. It's fair to assume that it is the profit engine and is where erosion of value would hit the bottom line the hardest.

Then, of course, a forward threat would be from agentic AI shopping assistants, or something to that effect. While PayPal will likely push a strategy to try to get its own foot into this world, we see threats from Google (GOOG), which is already trying to push sponsored products on you through its search engine as its longstanding primary business model and which is already multi-modal as well and has been for quite some time. Then, of course, they have Gemini, one of the foundational LLM models on the market that can already be prompted to advise purchasing decisions, though not carry them out for you. It also has Google Pay, another wallet competitor, and most people who would trust PayPal to intermediate their payment data would do the same with Google, in our view. Any of the companies behind a foundational model could ship a tool that is an agentic shopping agent, in our view. Anthropic has been testing a pilot marketplace with agents participating on behalf of buyers and sellers.

Cursory Valuation Discussion

We really see the utility of Agentic AI shopping, and for that reason we wouldn't want to take bets that could be clipped by the advancement of such utilities. PayPal, though once a darling, may not be an attractive enough fallen angel, despite very considerable forward FCFYs above 12%, which at least already price it for secular decline before it has in fact materialized. There is also the wonder of whether they can avoid secular decline entirely. Indeed, it's still a growing company, perhaps with more cards to play, but it's a victim of the AI-hedge trade that has hit SaaS as well very badly, and we don't feel they are in a position of strength, including with the general macro backdrop as well.

But there are high FCFYs in other parts of the payments world, though not quite as high as PYPL, admittedly. The reality is that more brick-and-mortar and retail-focused bets within payments processing or processing backend infrastructure, perhaps something like Nexi (NEXPF) (about 9% forward FCFY, so less value than PYPL), a beleaguered Worldline (WWLNF), or maybe a NA pick like Fiserv (FISV) (almost 7% forward FCFY), which we understand all have margin coming from PoS businesses and backend services, may have profits that are less indexed to the disruption of LLM-based shopping assistants. They are also maybe already further down in the commodification process, while PayPal is more recently starting to get pressure. Moreover, other than these disruptive competitive threats, in the interim there are also lateral competitive threats - things like Revolut Pay - that are chipping away at PayPal. Indeed, the fact that PYPL still may have farther to fall seems to be reflected in its even higher FCFY than some of these other picks.

That isn't to say these alternative, more traditional payment processing stocks, of which there are quite a few listed on the markets, don't have a lot of their problems. A lot of them have technical debt (in this case referring to inefficiencies or other weights on the business from non-integrated tech stacks resulting from acquisitions) from sprees of acquisitions reaching a climax in the low cost of capital period during COVID-19, also when shopping was going haywire. Adyen (ADYEY) is the only one that is fully native on its own stack, built independently and not cobbled together through acquisitions (hence the premium valuation, albeit still quite ample FCFY at 6% FWD 1 year, 8% FWD 2 as the sector still remains badly beaten by the market). Many of them are also getting commodified or regulated away from serving profitable but higher-risk vendors due to anti-money laundering pressure beyond just banks but on these payment processing companies too. Margins for these players often lie in PoS machines, where differentiation is difficult, and it's all about ephemeral customer appeal and hitting the PoS renewal cycles correctly. Growth is slowing there in general from highs and from macro, where the backdrop is deteriorating, but this is a concern for PayPal too. But they might be less exposed to these AI risks than PayPal and have less to lose from this point forward also in terms of competition and commodification.

Bottom Line

We are also attracted to those PYPL FCFYs that offer a lot of immediate shareholder flows. Particularly when there are areas at PYPL that are growing nicely, like Venmo (20% growth), which has Venmo Debit now, which will have decent takes, and the fact that these threats are still materializing, and that significant FCFY can generate a good amount of value in the interim, which is why we rate it a "hold" and not less - though maybe still not enough at current prices.

But with unusually high stakes driven by the branded checkout solution most at risk, lateral competition, disruptive competition down the line that we have to assume will eventually materialize, and, to our knowledge, no guarantee that PYPL's value proposition can endure indefinitely, we'd rather look for cheap ideas in less embattled parts of the market, where all this technological risk, which is impossible to really predict, and the timeline aren't an issue.

In our coverage universe, 12% FCFY is quite standard - you can find that sort of value even in traditional industrial sectors like waste management with similar growth rates or in healthcare services - areas where we don't worry about these tectonic shifts at all.
